title: The 10 Wildest Reboots in Movie History and ‘The Bride!’ Plus: A ‘Secret Agent’ Second Look and the Best Doc Contenders.
format: Sean Fennessey and Amanda Dobbins split over Maggie Gyllenhaal's 'The Bride,' trace a century of reboots and reimaginings, give 'The Secret Agent' a second look, and run all five Best Documentary nominees.

films.title: The Bride!
films.note: Maggie Gyllenhaal's third feature, an $80M Frankenstein-adjacent swing with Jessie Buckley and Christian Bale in 1930s Chicago. Sean found it messy but audacious and had more fun than he expected; Amanda bounced off the feminist-retelling premise and the Mary Shelley framing device (Buckley switching between Chicago flapper and cackling British narrator). Both agree the Me Too finale and tacked-on Sarsgaard/Cruz detective plot are a bungle. Tracking for $14-18M.

films.title: Bride of Frankenstein
films.note: James Whale's sequel, with Elsa Lanchester's wordless, shrieking bride: the wrong this movie sets out to correct by giving the character a point of view.

films.title: Frankenstein
films.note: Del Toro's version, where the bride is just Elizabeth; Sean weighs whether 'The Bride!' is really that much worse.

films.title: Babylon
films.note: Sean's closest comp: messy, movie-mad, metaphors on the nose, and he loves it.

films.title: Don't Worry Darling
films.note: A film stuck the same way: its thesis about a powerful female consciousness holds the genre back.

films.title: Joker
films.note: Same creative team (DP Lawrence Sher, composer Hildur Guðnadóttir); Sean sees the good parts of Joker here.

films.title: Joker: Folie à Deux
films.note: And the bad parts: the musical-misfire side of the comparison.

films.title: Birds of Prey
films.note: Amanda's touchstone for the greenish look and the 'girl-power reimagination' she's allergic to.

films.title: The Lost Daughter
films.note: Gyllenhaal's messy-woman debut; the thematic core she carried into a bigger-budget swing.

films.title: Star Wars: The Force Awakens
films.note: Sean's model for the right move: keep the IP, invent a new character (Rey), and just let her catch the lightsaber. No one has to say 'but she's a girl.'

films.title: Mickey 17
films.note: The direct box-office and critical comp ($133.5M global, 72 on Metacritic); another audacious swing that's too politically on the nose.

segments.heading: The 10 Wildest Reboots in Movie History

films.title: The Invisible Man
films.note: Sean's model for how to do it: Leigh Whannell and Elisabeth Moss bury the social import under a tight 90-minute thriller instead of announcing it.

films.title: The Mummy
films.note: Warner Bros.' April release with Jack Reynor: a modern-set story using the Karloff-era framework, the same old-IP move Universal's Dark Universe kept failing at.

films.title: Hook
films.note: Spielberg's grown-up Peter Pan; a childhood favorite Sean cooled on. Julia Roberts as Tinkerbell: 'Tinker Hell.'

films.title: The Fly
films.note: Cronenberg's 1980s riff, a good version of a reimagining that isn't a straight remake.

films.title: The Thing
films.note: Carpenter's riff on the 1951 film: same premise, its own beast.

films.title: The Thing from Another World
films.note: The original Carpenter riffed on.

films.title: Weird Science
films.note: Basically Bride of Frankenstein, brought to life by two horny teenage boys.

films.title: The Wiz
films.note: A reinvention of The Wizard of Oz.

films.title: The Wizard of Oz
films.note: The source The Wiz and countless others keep reworking.

films.title: You've Got Mail
films.note: A Shop Around the Corner update that also chains back to a Miklós László play: something based on something based on something.

films.title: The Shop Around the Corner
films.note: Lubitsch's film, itself adapted from László's Austrian play.

films.title: Cruella
films.note: The villain-reimagining strain: a feminist retelling pointed at the bad guy.

films.title: Snow White and the Huntsman
films.note: A 'not all men' retelling that makes the huntsman heroic.

films.title: Dracula Untold
films.note: Sean's 'clever and wildly unsuccessful' pick: Luke Evans' Vlad forced into monsterhood to save his nation, a sympathetic-villain origin.

films.title: The Blob
films.note: A mean 1980s remake of the McQueen original.

films.title: The Blob (1958)
films.note: The Steve McQueen original.

films.title: Invasion of the Body Snatchers
films.note: The first of four; the 'inherent feeling of being replaced' that keeps getting reimagined.

films.title: Invasion of the Body Snatchers (1978)
films.note: Philip Kaufman's version: more about 1970s paranoia.

films.title: Body Snatchers
films.note: Abel Ferrara's 1990s take.

films.title: The Invasion
films.note: The bungled Daniel Craig / Nicole Kidman fourth version.

films.title: Ponyo
films.note: A reimagining of The Little Mermaid, another attempt to fix the fairy tales. Currently the subject of an Academy Museum exhibit.

films.title: The Big Lebowski
films.note: Dashiell Hammett and The Long Goodbye recast against the low-level angst of the Gulf War.

films.title: The Long Goodbye
films.note: Altman's noir, itself part of the lineage Lebowski draws on.

films.title: Mirror Mirror
films.note: Tarsem's Snow White refocused on Julia Roberts' evil queen, the perspective-shift version.

films.title: Cinderella
films.note: The Lily James version that arrived around the same time as Mirror Mirror's fairy-tale wave.

films.title: Last Man Standing
films.note: Bruce Willis 1920s gangster remake of Yojimbo, not the Tim Allen sitcom.

films.title: Yojimbo
films.note: Kurosawa's original that Last Man Standing remade.

films.title: Halloween (2007)
films.note: Rob Zombie's first. Sean's take: bad.

films.title: Halloween II (2009)
films.note: Rob Zombie's second. Sean's take: good. Don't at him.

films.title: The Da Vinci Code
films.note: The running bit: a reimagining where the Holy Grail becomes the sacred feminine ('we fixed Christianity!'). Also, still fiction.

films.title: Rosaline
films.note: The Kaitlyn Dever Hulu movie about Romeo's ex, the sidelined-character retelling as comedy.

films.title: The Fall
films.note: Tarsem's other film, cited alongside The Cell as one of the most beautiful movies of the last 25 years. Now back in Julia Roberts' Letterboxd four.

films.title: The Cell
films.note: Tarsem's debut, the other half of Sean's beauty pairing.

films.title: Clueless
films.note: Filed under modern remake, not reimagining: the Emma update.

films.title: 10 Things I Hate About You
films.note: Same bucket: a modern-set remake (Taming of the Shrew), not a reclamation.

segments.heading: ‘The Secret Agent,’ a Second Look
segments.note: Kleber Mendonça Filho's Best Picture contender, now on Hulu

films.title: The Secret Agent
films.note: Wagner Moura carries a languid, phantasmagoric 1977 Recife thriller: Marcelo/Armando hiding out under the dictatorship, a shark-swallowed leg that goes on a spree, a framing device of two young women reviewing the historical record. Sean's third watch; both call it one of the wildest Best Picture nominees ever and are torn between it and Sentimental Value for International.

films.title: Jaws
films.note: The hovering god over the movie and its emotional key: Fernando's line that he was scared of it until he finally saw it, and then all the fear went away.

films.title: One Battle After Another
films.note: The season's recurring comp: two secret societies, revolution vs. control, and the long fallout.

films.title: I'm Still Here
films.note: The prior year's Brazilian-dictatorship film; more traditional and tightly focused, where this one sprawls. Both end on a memory-and-family flash-forward.

films.title: Pictures of Ghosts
films.note: Mendonça Filho's memory doc about Recife, probably better seen after The Secret Agent.

films.title: Bacurau
films.note: His earlier film with the same trick of turning into a horror movie for ten minutes, then stopping.

films.title: Sinners
films.note: The dual-role comp: Michael B. Jordan as Smoke and Stack; Moura effectively plays three (Marcelo, Armando, and son Fernando), each looking like a different person.

segments.heading: The Best Documentary Contenders
segments.note: all five premiered at Sundance; Sean and Amanda have concerns

films.title: The Alabama Solution
films.note: Andrew Jarecki and Charlotte Kaufman's six-year investigation of Alabama's prisons, built from footage the incarcerated men shot themselves. The one both agree earns its nomination (sturdy, classical, no ethical qualms), if a little information-dump over narrative.

films.title: Come See Me in the Good Light
films.note: Ryan White's film about poet Andrea Gibson's terminal diagnosis and their partner Megan Falley. Amanda respects the bravery but didn't want to sit in it; the medical-portal scenes landed hardest.

films.title: Cutting Through Rocks
films.note: Sara Khaki and Mohammadreza Eyni follow Sara Shahverdi, the first woman on her conservative Iranian village council. Fully observed, great access, a well-chosen subject, but Sean wanted more interrogation than slice-of-life.

films.title: Mr. Nobody Against Putin
films.note: David Borenstein and Pasha Talankin on a Russian school turned recruitment center. Amanda's bugaboo: named, identifiable students still in Russia while the vainglorious narrator smuggles himself out. Both find it self-serving.

films.title: The Perfect Neighbor
films.note: Gita Pullapilly's body-cam assembly of the Susan Lorincz shooting of Ajike Owens. Formally inventive and shattering (the scene of the children being told is the most pained Sean's been at a movie all year), but both are queasy about how it rubber-stamps the surveillance it indicts, and yada-yadas the Stand Your Ground stuff. The most-watched doc of the year on Netflix; won the Sundance directing award.

films.title: Cover-Up
films.note: Laura Poitras' Seymour Hersh portrait, the category's big snub. Sean loves that it shows the murky, error-prone reality of the work; suspects that honesty is why it missed.

films.title: 2000 Meters to Andriivka
films.note: The DGA winner Amanda would have taken over Mr. Nobody Against Putin.

films.title: My Undesirable Friends
films.note: A five-hour part-one that took Critics Awards; a deeper Cutting Through Rocks and a better film about being in Russia. Getting distribution April 3.

films.title: Mistress Dispeller
films.note: Sean's favorite of the also-rans: a fascinating, unscripted-but-unbelievable look at a Chinese custom of hiring someone to break up an affair. 'The bride of this group.'

films.title: Predators
films.note: A Sundance standout on the To Catch a Predator apparatus and its cultural knockdown effect; never got over the hump with audiences.

films.title: Zodiac Killer Project
films.note: A clever deconstruction: a filmmaker who lost the book rights narrates the true-crime doc he can't make. No chance in the category, but the kind of thing the branch should notice.

films.title: Megadoc
films.note: Sean's beloved making-of on Coppola and 'Megalopolis'; no making-of doc has ever won Best Doc.

films.title: Mr. Scorsese
films.note: A compelling, deep character portrait whose eligibility is fuzzy: conceived as a film, released as a miniseries.

films.title: Pee-wee as Himself
films.note: Matt Wolf's Paul Reubens doc, Sean's rejoinder to the celebrity-doc snobbery. Reubens dies mid-production and the film finishes without him.

films.title: Orwell: 2+2=5
films.note: Raoul Peck's not-great-but-interesting essay using Orwell's prescience, read by Damian Lewis.

films.title: André Is an Idiot
films.note: The tonal opposite of Come See Me in the Good Light: a colon-cancer diagnosis played as madcap unwinding.

films.title: My Mom Jayne
films.note: Mariska Hargitay's Jayne Mansfield doc, the PGA winner and not nominated: a sign of how scattered this year's precursors were.
